svg.text: How the server tells the client "Hey, talk to me at THIS address instead!"
This is the EXACT feature the QUIC-Exfil attack exploits
Imagine you call a company's main reception desk (PRIMARY).
"Actually, call me directly at my desk number
You hang up and call the direct number.
Same person, better connection, no need to re-introduce yourself!
1. Load Balancer --> Backend: Client first hits load balancer,
then gets redirected to actual server
2. CDN Optimization: Move client to closer edge server
3. Server Maintenance: Migrate clients off a server
The problem: This info is ENCRYPTED!
Firewalls don't know what the preferred address is.
So they can't verify if a migration is legit or fake!
PHASE 1: Handshake - Server secretly tells client the preferred address
FIREWALL CAN SEE: Initial packet, TLS ClientHello, SNI
This is the LAST thing the firewall can meaningfully read!
THE SECRET: preferred_address is HIDDEN inside the encrypted handshake!
ALL OF THIS IS ENCRYPTED! Firewall sees NOTHING!
Neqo code that SENDS preferred_address (server side):
PHASE 2: Client receives HANDSHAKE_DONE and finds the preferred address
The client receives a HANDSHAKE_DONE frame from the server. This means TLS handshake is complete.
At this exact moment, Neqo calls: self.migrate_to_preferred_address(now)
Check: Is preferred_address disabled in config?
Extract preferred_address from server's transport parameters
Save the new Connection ID the server gave us for this address
self.cids.add_remote(cid) // store CID for preferred address path
Pick the right address family (IPv4 or IPv6) to match current connection
This triggers the EXACT same migrate() flow -- creates new path, sends PATH_CHALLENGE!
PHASE 3: Client probes the preferred address (PATH_CHALLENGE)
[8 random bytes] + padding to 1200 bytes | CID from handshake
[same 8 bytes back] -- "Yes, I'm real, I got your message!"
C: Client validates! Path is GOOD!
ALL future traffic now goes to PREFERRED SERVER
Video, data, everything — same Connection ID, new destination IP
// This is the trigger! Right after handshake completes!
PHASE 4: What the FIREWALL sees (and why it's BLIND)
Firewall's view of all packets during server-side migration:
DESTINATION CHANGED! Client now talks to a different IP! But firewall doesn't know WHY!
192.168.1.50:43210 --> 10.0.0.99:443 | varies | All traffic to preferred address now
Firewall thinks: "Client started talking to a new IP. Probably a QUIC migration. Looks normal."
Firewall has NO WAY to know if 10.0.0.99 is a legitimate preferred address or an ATTACKER!
PHASE 5: THE QUIC-EXFIL ATTACK - How malware exploits this
The attacker does NOT need Neqo or any QUIC library!
They build RAW UDP packets that LOOK like a server-side migration. The firewall can't tell the difference.
- Waits for a CID to be RETIRED
Uses: pcap library + custom QUIC parser (Rust)
Malware builds a FAKE migration packet:
- Connection ID: retired CID from step 1
Can YOU tell which is real and which is the attack?
IDENTICAL from the outside. Both on Google Cloud. Both 1200 bytes. Both encrypted.
The firewall ALLOWS both. No way to distinguish. Attack succeeds.
ML Classifiers tried to detect this attack:
ZERO out of 5 vendors can detect QUIC connection migrations. 4 out of 5 recommend blocking QUIC entirely.
COMPLETE NEQO CODE MAP: Server-Side Migration (preferred_address)
Encodes PreferredAddress into transport parameters (only if Role::Server)
Serializes IPv4/IPv6/CID/SRT into wire format inside encrypted handshake
5. crypto.rs -- Encrypts everything. Sent inside EncryptedExtensions.
Extracts addr, picks IPv4/v6, calls self.migrate(None, Some(remote), false, now)
5. connection/mod.rs -- migrate() creates new Path, starts probing
Creates migration_target, sends PATH_CHALLENGE to preferred address
8. Server responds with PATH_RESPONSE --> path.rs: Paths::path_response()
Matches bytes, set_valid(), select_primary() --> MIGRATION COMPLETE!
THE KEY INSIGHT: Why this attack is so dangerous
The preferred_address is sent ENCRYPTED during the handshake.
No middlebox, firewall, or network observer can see what address the server told the client.
REAL: Client migrates to server's preferred address
Firewall sees: client talks to new IP. Allows it.
FAKE: Malware sends stolen data to evil server
Firewall sees: client talks to new IP. Allows it.
WHY NEQO MATTERS: It's the only open-source impl that fully supports this
COMPLETE TIMELINE: Server-Side Migration via preferred_address
Firewall CANNOT see anything from here on
Total: ~165ms from handshake to migrated. Completely invisible to middleboxes.
Based on: QUIC-Exfil paper (ASIA CCS '25) + Neqo v0.27.0 source code (mozilla/neqo)
QUICK REFERENCE: Key Files in Neqo for Server-Side Migration
connection/mod.rs:2070-2100 migrate() -- creates path, assigns CID, starts probe
path.rs:196-219 Paths::migrate() -- sets migration_target, starts probe
